data.title: Cisco’s Universal Quantum Switch Ushers in Quantum Networking
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

Cisco has unveiled the working prototype of a switch designed to transmit and relay quantum information between systems from different vendors, enabling interoperability.

At the heart of the switch is a patented quantum computing conversion engine capable of recognizing all encoding modalities at both input and output, the company claimed. Notably, the routing process preserves quantum information—a nontrivial requirement in quantum computing—and operates over standard telecom fiber infrastructure at room temperature.

In specific trials, the company reported preserving quantum information integrity, with fidelity degradation limited to 4% during encoding and entanglement.

Cisco said its first universal quantum switch test model forms a core component of its broader quantum networking framework, the result of years of research, extensive validation, and an expanding set of strategic collaborations.

Quantum states: superposition and entanglement

One of the key pillars of quantum computing and networking is the quantum bit (qubit), an extension of ordinary binary digits (bits) into the quantum domain. The two units of information conceptually diverge in that qubits represent information in the form of a linear superposition of quantum states, each one of them associated with a normalized probability amplitude; this means that the total probability is always 1, reflecting absolute certainty upon measurement.

Qubits can be implemented using a range of physical platforms, including superconducting circuits, quantum dots, trapped ions, photonic systems, and neutral atoms. While each approach may be at a different development stage of maturity with pros and cons, the goal of building a practical machine with millions of qubits remains distant.

Even more counterintuitive is entanglement, a quantum correlation between particles—including photons. This property is critical in quantum communication, enabling applications from distributed computing to cybersecurity.

In general, quantum information can be encoded in different ways—such as polarization, phase, or time-bin states of photons—depending on the architecture. However, this results in a lack of interoperability across different systems.

Retaining quantum information during routing is intrinsically a difficult task, as quantum states are highly sensitive to environmental noise (i.e., heat, electromagnetic waves, cosmic rays), which can induce decoherence. Overcoming these limitations while maintaining information integrity is therefore essential to the development of scalable, real-world quantum networks.

Unlocking the quantum era

Qubits are typically grouped into physical and logical qubits. A physical qubit is just a single piece of hardware, such as a neutral atom or a photon. Because of decoherence, quantum information can be lost in a fraction of a second. As a result, performing complex calculations on a single physical qubit is highly error-prone. The solution is to create “virtual” qubits called logical qubits, by grouping several physical qubits and using quantum error correction codes. The physical qubits in a group act collectively to store a unit of quantum information. By monitoring the interaction or correlation between these physical qubits (without disturbing their states), the system can detect if one of these qubits has failed and intervene to fix the error.

Today’s quantum computers do not have a sufficiently high number of logical qubits to address high-value applications in finance, healthcare, materials science, weather forecasting, or aerospace. In such environments, scientists estimate that it takes from 1,000 to 10,000 physical qubits to generate a single, error-free logical qubit.

Against this backdrop, Cisco is turning to quantum networking and connectivity to bridge this gap, a sort of replica of internet for the quantum world.

“We’ve long recognized that connecting quantum systems is the key to achieving true scalability, and now we’ve taken a critical step toward making that vision a reality”, said Vijoy Pandey, senior VP and general manager of Outshift, Cisco’s Emerging Technologies and Incubation Group.

The story repeats

The path toward quantum computers capable of tackling today’s most intractable problems does not necessarily depend on building ever-larger machines. A more likely approach is the interconnection of multiple quantum processors through dedicated quantum networks. That milestone is now approaching.

Classical computing faced a similar inflection point in the past, overcoming scaling limits by linking smaller processing nodes into distributed systems. Quantum computing appears to be following a comparable trajectory: Its full potential is unlikely to be realized through a single machine, but rather through distributed quantum data centers where processors interoperate over specialized networks.

In the new scheme, the universal quantum switch makes a difference. When two quantum computers—even from different suppliers—must share information, the universal quantum switch receives the signal in whatever modality it is encoded, converts it into a common code for routing, and transmits it in the format the receiving system needs, without losing any quantum information along the channel.

Supporting main quantum encoding modalities

In quantum networking, photons behave as high-speed carriers of quantum information, traversing long distances across fiber-optic cables and encoding fragile quantum states. Cisco said it has designed the quantum switch to support all major encoding modalities used to transmit this information:

  • Polarization (orientation of the electromagnetic field)
  • Time-bin (relative arrival times of photons)
  • Frequency-bin (color or frequency of light)
  • Path (physical route the photon takes)

Cisco opted for polarization to experimentally validate the switch’s functionality. This approach leverages the orientation of the electric field carried by the photon to define quantum states. Depending on whether the photon is polarized horizontally or vertically, we can define basis states “0” and “1”, so any quantum state can be expressed as a linear superposition of these basis states (or basis vectors in linear algebra) with coefficients corresponding to probability amplitudes.

However, optical fibers can modify polarization due to temperature fluctuations, bending, or structural imperfections. Active compensation is therefore required. Upon receiving photons, the switch must correctly route the encoded quantum state while preserving its coherence.

Moreover, Cisco incorporated time-bin and frequency-bin into the design, in preparation for the next validation step.

Entanglement chip and experimental results

Cisco’s quantum network is built around an entanglement chip, co-developed with the University of California, Santa Barbara. The chip generates pairs of entangled photons—typically through nonlinear optical processes—whose quantum states are strongly correlated.

Specifically, in one such nonlinear process, a single photon is converted into two lower-energy photons whose properties are linked by energy and momentum conservation laws. This results in a nonlocal joint quantum state in which the photons become entangled.

This distributed quantum resource is the basis for the transmission of quantum information: By allocating one of the two entangled photons to node A and the other to node B, the network automatically creates a nonlocal correlation between the nodes. Once such a link is established, any operation involving a qubit at node A instantaneously constrains the corresponding state of the qubit at node B: This is the coordination for quantum protocols.

The correlations established endure over long distances and support quantum teleportation, which refers to the transfer of a quantum state (not a physical particle), in conjunction with classical communication.

Tests with Cisco’s universal quantum switch confirmed basic requirements: Quantum information can be routed and processed across the ecosystem’s blocks quickly, reliably, and without degrading coherence. Moreover, the switch operates on a sub nanosecond timescale with the ability to reconfigure connections in a time interval as short as one nanosecond. Finally, power consumption is on the order of only 1 milliwatt.

The future of quantum networking

Quantum networking is a promising technology, albeit still in its infancy. Today’s current infrastructure can connect quantum systems that share the same encoding protocol, but Cisco said its universal quantum switch supports multiple modalities and runs on existing fiber-optic platforms.

Notably, it operates at room temperatures, unlike most of today’s quantum systems that require bulky and costly cryogenic refrigerators.

By allowing interoperability across different standards, Cisco said the universal quantum switch removes a key obstacle for deployment and frees potential users from single-vendor dependency.  

Integrated into Cisco’s full-stack strategy across hardware, software, and application layers, the universal quantum switch is being positioned as a component in the development of scalable, distributed quantum networks, with the aim of supporting progress toward practical quantum computing applications.

The Sovereign Quantum Race Starts Before Quantum Advantage

Race to Find the Next Nvidia in Quantum Computing

data.title: SUSE Launches Industrial Edge Platform Following Losant Acquisition
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

Germany-based open-source software provider SUSE has integrated Losant into its edge portfolio to build SUSE Industrial Edge, focused on cloud-native architecture, broad protocol support, and partner-led deployment across industries. The industrial IoT platform, developed following SUSE’s February 2026 acquisition of Losant, unifies operational data and enterprise intelligence across industrial environments.

In an interview with EE Times at SUSECON 2026, Keith Basil, VP and GM of the Edge Business Unit at SUSE, said the company aimed to fill a gap in its edge computing portfolio, where it previously lacked presence in the industrial tiny edge segment.

“The platform includes a low-code and no-code environment, allowing users to build workflows and applications quickly,” Basil said. “These workflows support dashboards that provide operational awareness across use cases such as maritime vessels, power plants, production lines, and retail stores. The platform also includes multi-level dashboards across sites, pre-built application templates, anomaly alerts, performance comparison tools, and integration with enterprise IT systems.”

The offering targets enterprises with fragmented IoT deployments across production facilities, stores, ships, or offices, where data is often siloed and requires manual correlation. It normalizes data from sources including Siemens, Beckhoff, HVAC systems, retail kiosks, computing systems, ship engines, and OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA) sources into a single view.

SUSE has combined the Losant platform with its runtime capabilities, with the runtime forming the base infrastructure layer and Losant capabilities layered on top. The integrated product was announced at SUSECON 2026 and will be offered under the SUSE Industrial Edge name.

Architecture and deployment model

Basil explained that the Losant platform operates as a Kubernetes application.

SUSE provides the runtime through its IT suite, which includes Linux and Kubernetes. The application layer sits above Kubernetes, forming a complete stack from operating system to dashboard. The underlying architecture uses independently scalable and stateless services, including NGINX load balancers, Mosca message brokers, RabbitMQ queuing systems, workflow runners, and data processors. Time-series data is stored using TimescaleDB, with additional storage from MongoDB document databases and Redis clusters. Basil said the system is designed with no single point of failure.

On industrial integration, Basil said protocol usage depends on the use case. “Since its start around 2015, Losant has expanded support to include OPC UA, MQTT [message queuing telemetry transport], Modbus, BACnet, and others. The platform now covers approximately 90% to 95% percent of industrial protocols,” he said.

For large-scale deployments, the company’s industrial edge platform supports both on-premises and cloud-based models. “In one model, a gateway collects data locally. In another, devices such as smart meters connect directly to the cloud. The platform can handle tens of thousands of devices distributed across regions, with dashboards showing device status and location while maintaining data separation between customers,” Basil explained.

He said observability is built into the system through protocols designed for low bandwidth and high latency environments. “SUSE uses MQTT in a publish-subscribe model, which supports large-scale device communication. The architecture addresses limited connectivity environments through localized design. Deployments operate within small, independent failure domains. A gateway edge agent handles protocol support and stores data locally using a time-series database. Workflows and dashboards run at the local level without external dependencies,” he elaborated.

Expanding on deployment approaches, Andrés Valero, technology advocate at SUSE, explained that SUSE supports multiple provisioning models depending on the edge environment. He added that SUSE also uses a “management as code” model based on Cluster API and GitOps workflows. In this setup, cluster definitions are stored in a Git repository, and any changes trigger automated updates across distributed environments. “Every time you change something in the Git repo, it propagates to the remote systems, updating configurations or upgrading clusters as needed,” he explained.

When connectivity is available, data follows an eventual consistency model and synchronizes with the cloud for storage and broader analysis. Basil revealed that SUSE Industrial Edge currently processes over 1.20 billion workflow transactions per month.

In a case study shared by the company, SUSE revealed that the Clark Construction Group had deployed Losant technology to monitor water systems at construction sites. The company installed flowmeters, pressure sensors, and control valves in domestic water mains connected to the platform. This provided visual monitoring of water flow and remote control through smart devices, helping prevent water damage and reduce insurance costs.

SUSE is also joining the Linux Foundation’s Margo Steering Committee to support open interoperability and standardization in industrial edge automation and plans to open source the Losant technology.

Valero shared that hardware flexibility is another part of the deployment strategy, with support across x86 and Arm architectures, as well as AI accelerators such as NVIDIA Jetson platforms. “Using SUSE’s Edge Image Builder, users can create customized system images that include AI frameworks and deploy them directly to edge devices, where they become operational immediately after boot,” he shared.

He added, “SUSE Linux Micro, an immutable and transactional operating system, is used as the foundation for these deployments, offering stability and security for edge and telecommunications use cases.”

Business model and partner strategy

SUSE plans to expand into multiple industries using a partner-led approach. The business model includes a partner fee that provides access to about 20 slots. Partners can price their offerings independently. “This allows partners to enter the industrial market quickly with fully branded solutions, while SUSE remains in the background,” Basil explained.

Basil said SUSE expects interest from global systems integrators as it expands its partner network. The company is yet to expand industrial partnerships in India. The Losant acquisition closed in late February, and the company has spent the past two months preparing productization and structuring stock keeping units (SKUs).

Security and future roadmap

To maintain consistency across partner-led deployments, SUSE uses a compliance-as-code approach. Basil said the company starts with strict security requirements, often based on U.S. government standards, and adapts them for other regions such as France and Germany or frameworks like the Network and Information Security Directive.

He described this as accelerated compliance, where SUSE preconfigures its components for secure deployment, while partners and customers complete additional requirements.

The company follows a “zero trust approach” according to Basil, where devices use encrypted communication with mutual Transport Layer Security, and connections require identity-based authentication. He explained, “SUSE applies best practices from Linux and Kubernetes, including Secure Technical Implementation Guides [STIGs] published by the Defense Information Systems Agency [DISA].”

Basil added, “SUSE uses secure enclaves where applications run in controlled environments. Security policies govern both incoming and outgoing traffic. We address governance, risk, and compliance requirements based on geography and industry, including standards such as Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act, Federal Risk and Authorization Management Program, and Federal Information Security Modernization Act.”

Looking ahead, the company is exploring AI-driven capabilities. Basil described a scenario where a user could request an AI agent to build a dashboard showing operational state and key performance indicators. The system would use an interface to the workflow engine and dashboard builder to generate the application based on the request. SUSE is positioning this within its broader cloud, edge, and AI portfolio, with integration into SUSE AI for intelligent edge applications.

See also:

SUSE Extends Single-Kernel Linux Strategy from Edge to Data Center

IoT for Industry 4.0: Benefits and Risks

data.title: SUSE Extends Single-Kernel Linux Strategy from Edge to Data Center
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

Germany-based open-source software provider SUSE is maintaining a single, scalable Linux kernel across systems ranging from edge devices to data center infrastructure, with potential future support for architectures such as RISC-V, said Matthias G. Eckermann, senior director of technology strategy at SUSE.

Enterprise Linux is being shaped by requirements spanning long lifecycle edge deployments, AI workloads, and supply chain verification.

Eckermann described a long-term architectural approach in which Linux remains consistent across systems from small edge devices to large-scale data center deployments. He added that this scalability could extend to additional processor architectures, including RISC-V.

“It is always the same kernel, it is the same libraries, it is the same tooling that you have on the Linux side,” Eckermann told EE Times, referring to SUSE’s approach to scaling Linux across device classes. He added that enterprise server support “from edge to data center” could extend “on RISC-V or whatever architecture you want,” while also stating that the company would remain focused on 64-bit systems due to the year 2038 limitation on 32-bit architectures.

Uniform kernel model across edge

Eckermann outlined a design model in which SUSE Linux is maintained as a single kernel baseline across deployments. He cited internal systems running SUSE Linux Enterprise Server 15 Service Pack 7. The same kernel, he said, is used in SAP HANA deployments and in systems scaling to thousands of virtual CPU cores.

The same approach, he said, applies across architectures, including Arm-based systems and large-scale Fujitsu platforms. “We scale from the minimum of one CPU to 8,192 CPUs with the same kernel,” he said.

He added that the approach has been developed over roughly three decades in collaboration with multiple hardware vendors, including Arm, Hewlett Packard Enterprise, IBM, and others.

Eckermann said the central mechanism for maintaining this consistency across architectures was SUSE’s Open Build Service. The system compiles packages once and distributes builds across supported platforms.

He said that while there is internal consideration of extending support to RISC-V, hardware maturity remains a constraint for large-scale build infrastructure. “There is no good hardware yet,” he said, referring to the requirements for handling tens of thousands of packages in server-grade environments.

Edge lifecycle pressures

Beyond architecture scalability, Eckermann highlighted what he described as conflicting requirements in edge computing environments. These include longer operational lifecycles compared with data centers, faster development cycles during deployment phases, and increased demand for immutable operating systems.

He said SUSE has moved toward integrating immutability capabilities into its enterprise Linux distribution, following earlier work with SUSE Linux Micro. In this model, the operating system remains stable while workloads change more frequently, with updates applied in controlled cycles.

At the infrastructure level, SUSE said it continues to use Multi Linux Manager for lifecycle management across distributed systems, including point-of-sale deployments that the company has supported for around 20 years.

Eckermann also described an expansion beyond traditional infrastructure management into what he termed an “edge workflow layer,” where customer business logic and application workflows are managed alongside operating system and cluster infrastructure.

This includes integration with Kubernetes-based systems, which SUSE has supported for around eight years, as well as engagement with low-code and no-code tooling approaches.

Discussing customer deployment assumptions, Eckermann pointed to bandwidth variability across regions as a factor influencing edge management strategies. He contrasted high-bandwidth environments with regions where connections may be limited to around 16 megabits.

“In cloud and on-premises, image-based deployments work well,” he said. “On the edge, bandwidth requirements are often underestimated. Image-based updates may not always be suitable for edge environments. Package-based updates may offer more granular control in constrained networks, including satellite systems.”

Eckermann highlighted onboarding and remote update challenges in distributed environments where on-site technical support may be limited. “Without secure onboarding and update mechanisms, edge devices risk becoming indistinguishable from general-purpose computing systems without operational safeguards,” he said. “Secure onboarding mechanisms, including FIDO-based approaches, are being integrated, but remain an ongoing area of development.”

Supply chain security and reproducible builds

Eckermann described SUSE’s Open Build Service as a foundation for supply chain traceability. Source files are stored with hashes, and builds are executed in isolated kernel-based virtual machine environments without network access.

“All dependencies are explicitly defined,” he said, noting that build outputs are also hashed and tracked through software bills of materials.

Reproducible builds allow external verification of software outputs, even if not widely used in practice. However, they require consistency across machines, architectures, toolchains, and timing. Eckermann said SUSE has removed variables such as timestamps and host names in newer builds to maintain determinism.

On long-term support models, Eckermann explained the company’s approach to backporting patches from open-source codebases, including those aligned with OpenELA. “Quality assurance processes are used to ensure API stability and functional consistency during updates,” he said.

To further strengthen trust models, Eckermann suggested distributed build verification across multiple regions. “Open source and reproducible builds serve as mechanisms for establishing trust across borders rather than replacing regulatory frameworks,” he said.

See also:

Turning RISC-V Standards Into Products That Ship

RISC-V Exceeding Expectations in AI, China Deployment

data.title: Why Chip Sovereignty Is No Longer About Chips—But Systems
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

Governments around the world are pouring tens of billions into semiconductor manufacturing in the name of chip sovereignty. From the U.S. to Europe to Asia, the assumption is clear: Control the fabs, and you control the future of computing.

But as AI reshapes the semiconductor landscape, that assumption no longer captures where value is created. Strategic advantage is shifting toward a broader—and more complex—stack: AI accelerators, high-performance CPUs, advanced packaging, memory bandwidth, and the software and data that tie them together.

The result is a growing disconnect between how policymakers define strategy and how the industry actually builds and deploys AI. In practice, industry is moving from a chip-centric model to a system-centric one.

From chips to systems

According to Mark Papermaster, CTO of AMD, the shift has happened faster than most expected. “The way people think about strategic leadership in AI has really changed as compared to just a year ago,” he told EE Times. “AI used to be thought of as associated with a GPU. But over the last year, it’s become clear that you need extensive computation across a much broader system.”

The system is no longer defined by any single class of chips. Instead, it spans CPUs, GPUs, specialized accelerators, memory, storage, and networking—working together to support increasingly complex workloads. The biggest change is the rise of what Papermaster describes as agentic workflows—AI-driven workflows that not only generate outputs but orchestrate entire sequences of tasks across enterprise software, databases, and applications.

“You’re running existing computing—CRM, ERP, databases—but now it’s working in conjunction with GPUs and AI accelerators,” he said. “AI is being deployed across a vast expanse of point applications.”

Control of individual components is no longer sufficient. What matters is how they are integrated into functioning systems. This shift plays to the strengths of U.S.-based firms, which have long led in system architecture, chip design, and software rather than manufacturing alone.

Seen through this lens, a new hierarchy of strategic technologies is coming into focus—one that extends well beyond individual chips. At the top are AI accelerators and GPUs, which drive both training and inference. Alongside them sit high-performance CPUs, which orchestrate workloads and run general-purpose computation.

But below that chip level is where much of the differentiation is shifting. Advanced packaging technologies, including chiplets and 3D stacking, are enabling multiple types of compute to be integrated into a single system. At the same time, memory bandwidth has emerged as a critical constraint, particularly for inference when context windows are large.

“Computation is moving heterogeneous,” Papermaster said. “It’s not just one type of computation—it needs different mixes based on the workload.”

Chiplet architectures, pioneered by AMD and now widely adopted, are a key enabler of this model. By breaking processors into smaller components that can be combined in different configurations, chiplets allow designers to optimize for specific applications while increasing flexibility in manufacturing.

Advanced packaging now determines how efficiently these chiplets can be connected, how power is delivered, and how heat is managed—factors that directly affect performance and scalability. This is not just an engineering issue. It is also a geopolitical one.

Chris Miller, author of “Chip War” and professor at Tufts University, sees packaging as one of several areas whose importance has risen alongside AI. “Shifts in the technology undergirding AI systems have brought to the foreground advanced packaging as an area that is very much in focus right now,” he said.

Much of today’s advanced packaging capacity is concentrated in Asia, particularly in Taiwan and South Korea, reflecting the region’s leadership in both manufacturing scale and packaging integration—even as the U.S. and Europe ramp up investment to build domestic capabilities.

The hidden chokepoints

If advanced packaging is a visible shift in the AI stack, Miller points to a less visible—but equally critical—layer: the underlying inputs that make semiconductor production possible.

In practice, chip manufacturing depends on a vast network of materials, chemicals, gases, and specialized components, many of which are produced by a small number of suppliers. “If you dig into supply chains, what you’ll find is that many of the most vulnerable chokepoints are actually in the subcomponents and the materials,” Miller said.

This exposure cuts across regions. Even U.S. chip design leaders depend on globally distributed inputs, underscoring how deeply interconnected the supply chain has become.

These chokepoints aren’t always where the most economic value is captured—but they can determine whether entire segments of the industry function at all. “If you don’t have them, you’re in trouble,” Miller said. The number of such chokepoints is relatively small, but their strategic importance is high, especially if they can be disrupted or weaponized.

This balance between chokepoints and value capture helps explain how regions should think about their position in the global supply chain. Rather than trying to replicate every part of the ecosystem, Miller argues that countries should build on their existing strengths while addressing specific vulnerabilities.

That perspective applies globally. In the U.S., it raises questions about how far to push for domestic manufacturing versus maintaining access to global supply chains. In Asia, it highlights both the concentration of key capabilities—such as advanced packaging—and the risks that come with that concentration.

Nowhere, however, is this tension more visible than in Europe, which is often portrayed as lagging due to its lack of leading-edge fabs. But that framing, Miller argued, reflects a broader misunderstanding of how the semiconductor industry works on a global scale.

“Europe’s a very strong player in some highly important and highly profitable parts of the semiconductor ecosystem,” he said, pointing to tools, materials, and advanced research. “Policymakers often look and say there’s not a lot of advanced fabrication; therefore, it’s a weak ecosystem. In fact, I think the opposite conclusion is true.”

Rather than replicating the full supply chain, regions should focus on mitigating specific vulnerabilities while reinforcing existing strengths. “The best strategy is to do what you’re already doing well and make sure you keep doing it well,” Miller said.

From an industry perspective, that specialization aligns with how modern compute systems are built. Papermaster emphasized that resilience comes not from self-sufficiency, but from diversity and openness across the supply chain. No country, he noted, can realistically replicate the entire semiconductor ecosystem within its own borders, making diversification across regions essential.

Rethinking strategic investment

Together, these perspectives challenge the notion that semiconductor leadership must be defined by manufacturing scale alone and suggest a more targeted approach to industrial policy. Regions should:

  • Invest in system-level performance, not just manufacturing
  • Secure access to critical supply chain inputs
  • Avoid duplicating what the global supply chain already does well

For policymakers in all regions, the implication is the same: Leadership should depend less on controlling individual steps and more on positioning within a global ecosystem. In other words, strategy should be guided not by a desire for completeness, but by a clear understanding of where value—and vulnerability—actually lie.

Ultimately, the shift underway in semiconductors is not just technological. It is conceptual. Sovereignty is no longer about controlling a single step in the value chain. It is about ensuring access to—and influence over—a complex, interconnected ecosystem.

That stack spans chips, packaging, software and data. It is distributed across regions, companies, and technologies—and evolving rapidly as AI reshapes the demands placed on compute.

In the AI era, the question is no longer simply who can build chips. It’s who can build—and ultimately control—the full stack of systems, AI models and software that make those chips matter.

data.title: Telecom Industry Bets on Automation to Tackle AI Squeeze
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

Telecom equipment manufacturers and cellular providers are caught in an unforgiving economic cycle. As the global semiconductor supply chain pivots to feed the booming demand for AI hardware, telecom vendors face soaring component costs while simultaneously grappling with shrinking customer budgets.

To survive this margin squeeze, the industry is accelerating a strategic shift toward advanced software automation.

Capex and revenue crunch

Compounding these inflationary pressures is a stark reality on the demand side: Telecom operators are tightening their belts. Global telecom capital expenditures are projected to decline by 2% in 2026. 

Stefan Pongratz, VP at Dell’Oro Group, told EE Times that “2026 will be a challenging year for the telco suppliers, especially those with a stronger wireless focus.” Explaining the pullback, Pongratz noted that “mobile data traffic is not growing as quickly as in the 4G era, and 5G is also providing a greater capacity boost.”

Without explosive growth to justify aggressive spending, giants such as Verizon have reduced 2026 capital expenditures to between $16 billion and $16.5 billion, down from $17 billion in 2025. Telefonica similarly aims to lower its capital intensity to 11% by 2030.

Supply chain shock

As budgets shrink, input costs are skyrocketing. The reallocation of foundry capacity toward AI processors has unleashed severe cost inflation across broader chip categories.

Spot prices for memory modules critical to base stations and consumer premises equipment have surged by more than 600% in recent months, with fourth-quarter contract prices jumping nearly 80%. In some low- to mid-tier routers, memory now accounts for over 20% of the total bill of materials, up from just 3% a year earlier.

The toll on corporate profitability is already evident. Swedish infrastructure provider Ericsson recently reported a staggering 79% plunge in net income to 887 million Swedish kronor (US$97 million) for the first quarter of 2026, alongside a 10% decline in net sales.

The company noted that sales in North America declined due to a short-term reallocation of customer spend and accelerated investments in the prior year. While currency exchange rates and restructuring expenses contributed to the decline, the company specifically identified component inflation as a primary factor affecting profitability.

CEO Börje Ekholm explicitly linked the margin pressure to the semiconductor squeeze, stating the “AI boom is increasing input costs.” Competing for limited foundry capacity against massive cloud providers leaves telecom vendors with diminished negotiating leverage.

Furthermore, the emerging buzz around AI-integrated radio access networks (AI-RAN) offers no immediate financial relief. Pongratz observed that these investments are currently changing the mix of operator spending rather than growing the overall capital expenditure budget.

Automation as the strategic imperative

Faced with this dual threat of surging hardware costs and a stagnant revenue pool, vendors and operators are making software automation their strategic imperative.

“The slower the growth, the higher the pressure,” Pongratz explained. “So, if operator revenue continues to grow around 1% annually and mobile/fixed data traffic continues to grow while network complexities/energy costs are rising, there is more pressure to automate.”

The industry is aggressively transitioning toward advanced autonomous networks, such as Level 4 (L4) Autonomous RAN.

“L4 is not just about cutting opex—it is about the pressure to automate in a slow-growth world,” Pongratz stated. He further elaborated that “if operator revenue continues to grow around 1% annually and mobile/fixed data traffic continues to grow while network complexities/energy costs are rising, there is more pressure to automate.”

While mid-sized operators may not pioneer these deployments, Pongratz noted that “the operators agree the end destination is automation with minimal human intervention”.

Navigating the 6G paradox

Looking ahead to the 6G transition, this architectural shift presents a structural paradox. Embedding AI capabilities directly into the radio access network requires the same highly sought-after semiconductor components that are currently experiencing price spikes.

Because cell sites operate under strict cost controls and tight power budgets, deploying power-intensive compute hardware is difficult to justify financially. This cost-power constraint could slow the rollout of AI-enabled 6G infrastructure, causing operators to weigh investments carefully.

Concerns persist regarding the performance-per-watt gap between general-purpose GPUs and custom silicon for these specific workloads. To offset the cost of new hardware and manage power consumption, vendors are developing software to improve operational efficiency and spectral utilization.

While mid-sized operators are unlikely to pioneer these deployments immediately, Pongratz pointed out that “the operators agree the end destination is automation with minimal human intervention.” Rather than a sudden shift, the industry expects “gradual progress, followed by faster adoption once the early adopters show the ROI,” he argued. Ultimately, the long-term viability of telecom infrastructure providers hinges on mastering this automation, delivering leaner, hyper-efficient networks to operators that can no longer afford to spend their way to growth.

data.title: Cyient Semiconductors’ Power Play: How Kinetic Technologies Fits the Puzzle
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

Cyient Semiconductors, a Hyderabad, India-based fabless custom ASIC firm, has pursued a series of strategic partnerships and acquisitions since its official launch in April 2025, including the acquisition of Kinetic Technologies. About a year after spinning off from parent company Cyient to focus exclusively on semiconductors, it has taped out more than 25 ASICs and shipped millions of units.

Cyient Semiconductors has sealed several ecosystem partnerships, including (but not limited to):

  • Co-development with Navitas on GaN products for the Indian market
  • Co-development with MIPS for custom RISC-V-based ASICs
  • Channel partnership with GlobalFoundries as an authorized reseller
  • Partnership with Anora to build a semiconductor validation and production test facility
  • Majority stake acquisition in Kinetic Technologies

These moves reflect a larger scheme to position the company—India’s largest custom silicon provider—in intelligent, efficient silicon power conversion solutions. A recent conversation with Suman Narayan, CEO of Cyient Semiconductors, outlined the rationale behind the approach: “We’re building a domain-optimized intelligent power platform that scales across applications.”

In other words, the ASIC company is expanding its reach and expertise in four key blocks—MCU/CPU, power, analog mixed-signal, and RF—with a particular recent focus on power.

The full interview can be viewed below:

Majority stake in Kinetic

Perhaps the most striking of all these moves is the acquisition of a majority stake in Kinetic Technologies for $85 million, down from the $93 million originally announced. With 100+ patents and 250 application-specific standard products (ASSPs) focused on power management IP, Kinetic brings a strong portfolio in power conversion. Kinetic generates tens of millions in revenue annually, with a total of 3 billion parts sold under its port protection solutions alone.

“It really positions us as a custom ASSP company primarily around DC-to-DC conversion on the low-voltage side,” said Narayan in a separate, recent interview focused on the Kinetic closing. The deal potentially doubles their serviceable addressable market to $8.5 billion, targeting both low- and high-voltage segments.

Kinetic Technologies is a nearly 20-year-old analog and mixed-signal company in San Jose, California, with merchant silicon spanning surge-protection ICs, USB data-line protection ICs, motor-control ICs, EMI/ESD suppression, DC/DC conversion solutions, and more. At first glance, the company seems to follow a more traditional catalog ASSP model that diverges slightly from Cyient Semiconductors’ custom ASIC model.

However, Narayan sees it as a custom-ASSP play. “They know the market, and they’re building custom chips for that market,” he said. Ultimately, he sees the unison as a method to tighten three key aspects of building any custom ASSP or ASIC for both companies: reducing BOM costs, increasing product integration, and improving power efficiency.

“The customer wants IP integrated, and we’re happy to do that integration,” said Narayan, elaborating on the reasoning behind the majority Kinetic stake. “We’re still straddling the question of where the ASICs go across multiple markets, while the ASSPs go after a fixed-power market—in this case, the low-voltage power-protection market.”

The Kinetic Technologies team can draw on engineering talent from AnSem, acquired by parent company Cyient in 2018 and folded into Cyient Semiconductors at spinoff. The AnSem team has deep roots at imec, enabling Cyient Semiconductors to build on its mixed-signal expertise with the Leuven, Belgium-based design center.

The expanded talent pool could strengthen Kinetic’s operations by adding an agile analog and power team. Narayan continued, “It’s first pass silicon or bust for them [Kinetic]; they’re a good machine, and we can now help with some of the engineering prowess.” Narayan added that Cyient Semiconductors was also providing access to their sales team and connections with large distributors. “We don’t want to disturb the DNA [of Kinetic]. We want to enhance it.”

Access to an expanded customer base is equally significant, giving Cyient Semiconductors a foothold in regions where it had no prior presence and a path into Asian OEM supply chains through Kinetic’s decade-long relationships in the region. “If you look at Taiwan, Korea, China—the GEOs that we’ve been absent in—it kind of complements our GEO expansion,” said Narayan, noting that there were overlaps in Europe.

‘A competitive position in terms of power.’

Cyient Semiconductors is aggressively expanding in the power semiconductor space. During our interview, Narayan noted that the market stood at $44 billion. He sees Kinetic as a credible point of entry for the low-voltage space, while the company builds out its portfolio in the high-voltage and medium-voltage. “We’ll have all the pieces of the puzzle together over time: driver, controller, and GaN, or driver, controller, and FET.”

The power electronics industry has shifted a great deal of its spotlight to data center power delivery, from the grid down to the core, as demand for AI workloads soars. The near- to medium-term goal is to revamp the data center power infrastructure to eliminate all unnecessary losses, prompting data center operators to rethink power distribution. As a result, there is a massive demand and a subsequent vacuum for suitable data center power-conversion technology that silicon vendors are eyeing.

Data centers are one such use case that Cyient Semiconductors has its sights on as well, with a focus on conversion stages from 800 down to 6 V. “Our goal is to primarily focus on that front-end of the equation,” Narayan said.

He touched upon this during the above-referenced interview, addressing three major challenges in data centers:

  • Microsecond transient responses through fast-switching, digital control, and protection
  • Grid-to-grid power integrity with control architectures that monitor peak currents across AC and DC
  • Fault protection to prevent instability during power-collapse events

“On the low voltage side, we want to double down with the Kinetic team, who played in the protection side of things—voltage and current protection—both on the head end as well as on the protection side,” Narayan said. In other words, Kinetic can assist with this piece of the puzzle as well, with IP protection guarding against overvoltage events, surges, and fault conditions from the grid, as well as ongoing current and voltage monitoring at each conversion stage.

The short-term goal seems to be centered on ensuring that Kinetic generates enough revenue and profit to justify the purchase and any integration costs. “We’re driving to get the EPS [earnings per share] accretive by year two, so I think we’re positioning ourselves in the right direction in terms of being in a competitive position in power,” Narayan said.

See also:

Cyient Semiconductors to acquire Kinetic Technologies for $93 million

Inside Cyient Semiconductors Bet on RISC-V for Custom Silicon

data.title: TSMC Unfolds Map for Process, Packaging Tech
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

TSMC just gave a peek at its chip roadmap for new process and packaging technology, including a photonics solution that promises to cut power consumption and reduce latency in AI data centers.

SVP Kevin Zhang briefed reporters and analysts a day before the opening of TSMC’s annual technology symposium, starting in California and continuing later this year around the world.

The company announced two derivatives of A14, its 1.4-nm, gate-all-around (GAA) process starting in 2028 that’s designed for AI data centers and edge devices. The A14 enhancements, A13 and A12, will begin production in 2029. A13 will reduce chip size by about 6% compared with A14, the company said.

TSMC also said N2U, a derivative of its world-leading 2-nm process, will start production in 2028, reducing power consumption by as much as 10%.

TSMC is trying to catch up with a surge in demand for AI chips that has helped surpass earlier expectations for chip production to reach $1 trillion by 2030. The semiconductor industry crossed the trillion-dollar threshold this year, Zhang said.

“By 2030, we’re saying the overall market revenue will exceed $1.5 trillion,” Zhang said. “The largest contribution will come from high-performance computing and AI, at over 55%.”

TSMC’s comments on advanced packaging, which integrates memory and logic chiplets into a single, high-performance package, were particularly noteworthy, according to Dan Hutcheson, vice chair at TechInsights.

Reviving Moore’s Law

“I saw their system-integration density gains as bringing life back to Moore’s Law,” Hutcheson told EE Times. “[Intel founder Gordon] Moore’s 1965 paper called for a doubling every year. That’s what TSMC clocked in at between 2024 and 2026. This, combined with the 6% lithographic density gain on A13, signals that advanced packaging is displacing lithography as the key enabler of density gains.”

For now, TSMC doesn’t need ASML’s most-advanced high-NA EUV lithography tools to make chips at the world-leading 2-nm and finer process nodes. Intel has adopted high-NA EUV equipment yet still lags TSMC in the advanced nodes.

“I’m amazed by our R&D team,” Zhang said. “They continue to find a way to drive technology scaling without using high-NA. One day, they may have to use it, but at this point, given the technology, with A14, A13, we continue to be able to harvest the benefits from current EUV, and not have to go to high-NA, which is very expensive.”

TSMC has been using its multi-patterning knowhow on its existing base of ASML low-NA tools as a workaround to make advanced-node chips ranging from 7 nm to 2 nm. One high-NA EUV tool costs about $380 million, according to Taiwan news source TrendForce.

“TSMC’s lack of a plan for high-NA EUV adoption was not a surprise,” Hutcheson said. “The issue they face is customer pushback on its field size being cut in half. It’s another signal that advanced packaging is displacing lithography as the key enabler of density gains. At least until the mask industry can develop a larger reticle.”

The size of a reticle limits the area that lithography tools can print in a single exposure. AI chipmakers are forced to overcome the reticle limit by making multiple chiplets that are combined in one 3D package.

One of TSMC’s disadvantages compared with rival Intel is its lack of backside power delivery in GAA chips. TSMC won’t start production of its super power rail tech until 2027 with the A16 process. Intel was first to bring backside power delivery into production in 2024.

Advanced packaging

Zhang showed a diagram of how TSMC sees the 3D architecture of chips evolving.

The diagram outlines TSMC’s new COUPE (Compact Universal Photonics Engine), entering production later this year, as well as a scheme to couple faster, higher density memory with compute logic.

This year, TSMC will roll out a 5.5 reticle-sized interposer it will use to integrate 12 HBM chips together with logic.

“Next year, we’re going to extend our CoWoS interposer size all the way to a 9.5× reticle, and in 2028, it will be 14×,” Zhang said.

As advanced packaging continues to evolve, the interposer can be a limitation, he noted.

“Ultimately, we’re going to use the so-called system-on-wafer (SOW) to bring more logic and compute units together,” he said. “You don’t need a substrate anymore. If you think about the whole wafer, a 300-mm wafer, it’s actually equivalent to a 40× reticle size when you measure by CoWoS or interposer technology.”

Cerebras is among the first to use SOW tech, making “somewhat unique” inferencing silicon, Zhang said.

“They already leveraged our wafer-level integration to bring so many compute die together,” Zhang added. “You basically bring the whole wafer as your base to integrate all the die. From a cost/performance perspective, it’s pretty competitive technology.”

CoWoS crunch ending

TSMC sees short supplies of its main advanced-packaging tech, CoWoS, ending soon.

“Our CoWoS capacity has grown substantially to reach a level to be able to address our customer demand at this point,” Zhang said.

TSMC will combine CoWoS with COUPE to bring the interposer closer to the switching die in AI chips.

“By doing that, you can achieve another 2× of latency reduction,” Zhang said. “At the same time, you actually improve power efficiency by about 2.5×. This is the ultimate integration technology we’re going to develop to provide better chip-to-chip communication in the future to address AI applications.”

TSMC follows smaller foundries like GlobalFoundries and Tower, which entered the silicon photonics business more than a year ago.

TSMC expects to continue 30% energy efficiency improvement per generation of chips.

“Every two years, we drive 30% efficiency,” he said.

Those efficiency improvements won’t necessarily be the same in the AI data centers that use TSMC chips, Zhang added.

“At the data center level, when you connect hundreds of thousands of GPUs or AI accelerators together, you need to have a lot of additional components,” Zhang told EE Times. “You have switches, power supply management, you have lots of things. The overall power benefit obviously has to be measured ultimately at the data-center level.”

The flurry of announcements from TSMC doesn’t necessarily signal that the company is accelerating the arrival of new process nodes, Hutcheson said.

“I’m not so sure that this is an acceleration of real new node introduction cadence as it is a willingness to be open and confident about releasing new nodes,” Hutcheson said. “The industry has had to deal with a lot of process complexity lately: GAA, backside power, new materials, in addition to simple scaling. I’d say they are getting this under more control and so are more confident about releasing their roadmap.”

See also:

Taiwan’s Tech Industry

TSMC 3-nm Upgrade in Japan to Catch up With Demand

data.title: Canada’s Defense Sector Talks Up Quantum
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

Quantum technologies are foundational to modern defense and economic competitiveness, and Canada’s defense sector isn’t waiting for them to mature somewhere else in the world.

At a recent luncheon roundtable held at Canada’s Empire Club in downtown Toronto, the intersection of quantum and Canada’s defense sector was discussed by panelists representing industry and the country’s military.

“Allied nations are integrating quantum sensing, secure communications, and advanced computing into their core missions,” Saurabh Popat, chair of the Empire Club Board of Directors, said as he introduced the panel participants.

He said the shift from concept to capability is happening, as evidenced by North American Aerospace Defense Command (NORAD) modernization, NATO’s emerging quantum strategy, and Canada’s recently released Defense Industrial Strategy (DIS).

Defense will be the earliest adopter of quantum technologies because the advantages are too significant to ignore, Popat said. “If we do not convert Canada’s quantum strengths and advantage into deployable capability and industrial scale, they will be captured elsewhere along with the talent, IP, and economic value that will follow.”

Lisa Lambert, CEO of Quantum Industry Canada, said the DIS marks a significant shift in national policy, tightly linking sovereignty, security, and industrial capacity, with quantum technologies named as sovereign capabilities. “Within the strategy, quantum sensing, communications and computing are named as sovereign capabilities.”

She added that the word “quantum” appears 12 times in the document: “This is not accidental. It’s a demand signal, and demand signals carry obligations.”

Quantum will be an early indicator of how this broader shift unfolds regarding how Canada translates strategic intent into operational and industrial capacity, Lambert said. “Canada is moving from assuming security to engineering it, from observing industrial opportunity to building it, from inventing technologies to anchoring them.”

Although Canada boasts a globally leading quantum ecosystem, Lambert said it faces a persistent challenge of commercializing and retaining innovation domestically. With the right alignment, however, she said the country can capitalize on quantum for both defense and economic prosperity.

Lambert said continuous engagement among government, military, and industry is essential, as well strategic investment that mirrors efforts by allies such as the U.S. and the U.K., citing the Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency’s (DARPA’s) Quantum Benchmarking Initiative, which has Canadian participants, and recently announced Canadian Quantum Champions Program.

Procurement must match technology maturity and support rapid deployment, Lambert said, and Canada is procuring quantum technologies today. “Quantum is inherently dual-use,” she said. “Many capabilities will be adopted first in defense because the missions demand it, but the value creation will extend across the entire economy.”

Lieutenant-General Darcy Molstad, the first Commander of Canadian Joint Forces Command (CJFC), which was formally established in late 2025, is tasked with engaging with industry to effectively leverage quantum and other advanced technologies. CJFC is a new organizational layer intended to make joint operations more coherent and responsive and better integrate capabilities such as cyber, space, intelligence, and multidomain operations under a single command structure.

Molstad said Canada’s new DIS demonstrates political will at all levels for collaborative engagement and a sense of urgency around operationalizing new capabilities and fostering industry-military partnerships. “We absolutely need the capabilities, and we need them yesterday,” he said.

Over the last two years, Molstad said there’s been a shift in the Canadian military’s approach to engaging with industry: “We have to work together on the problems and challenges.” He said it’s not realistic for him to write a statement of requirements defining military needs five years from now. “I need to tell you what my gaps are, what my challenges are, and we need to work together on those solutions and work together to operationalize them quickly.”

Molstad said a current focus for him is setting up a “rapid capabilities unit” within his organization that thinks and does things differently, including adding advisory capabilities on deep technology and venture capital.

He said quantum technologies for defense is an area where Canada can lead so it can demonstrate to allies that it is a net contributor, not a net consumer, of security and defense technologies. “We have to have our eyes wide open, but we have an opportunity to do some true nation building in every sector, not just quantum,” Molstad said. “We need moonshots.”

Quantum in Canada also needs significant amounts of external financing, said Francesco Bova, associate professor at the University of Toronto’s Rotman School of Management. “There’s other deep-tech domains where you could imagine bootstrapping your way to commercialization. We will not be bootstrapping our way to building a fault-tolerant quantum computer.”

Bova said raising capital in quantum is different than raising capital for other domains in the venture capital community because it often lacks recurring revenue. “Much of the domain is still within the R&D phase.”

David Roy-Guay, founder and CEO of SBQuantum, said a challenge for quantum startups like his is gaining access to real-world field deployments. The company has developed a novel quantum magnetometer that enables GPS-independent navigation in denied, degraded, or contested environments and by detecting magnetic anomalies associated with hidden objects and infrastructure.

Roy-Guay said the technology was designed in Canada but built elsewhere. He said it’s critical for SBQuantum and other companies to be able to deploy in realistic environments and gather feedback. “Once we demonstrate there’s really a problem to be solved there and that our technology can do better, then we can go around the supply chain and build the capabilities and do much more in Canada,” he said.

Roy-Guay’s advice for the emerging quantum companies is to start small and simple: “Get down to the end users, demonstrate what needs to be built, and once you have a big enough market, build it in-house.”

Don’t focus on perfection, he added. “That’s not going to make the difference for the end users. Cultivate your agility as you start so that you can deliver quicker than even the biggest companies all around the world.”

See also:

Optimism Drives Canada’s Semiconductor Scaling Efforts

Canada Eyes AI Opportunities at the Edge

data.title: EU DARE Project Is Scrambling to Replace Codasip
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

The European High-Performance Computing Joint Undertaking (EuroHPC JU), an organization advancing European supercomputing, and the Digital Autonomy with RISC-V in Europe (DARE) consortium, an initiative backed by a €240 million budget, are actively managing the implications of a corporate realignment by Codasip, one of their project’s partners.

Munich-based Codasip recently announced a strategic pivot toward cyber-resilient architectures and the divestment of specific business units to an undisclosed U.S.-based public semiconductor company. This development introduces variables into the architectural and financial planning of the DARE project, which focuses on developing processors and accelerators for European data centers.

Codasip, DARE, and EuroHPC, contacted by EE Times, did not respond to requests for comments regarding the specifics of the divestment or the implications for the project. EE Times, however, collected reactions from other stakeholders across the ecosystem.

Codasip’s strategic realignment

Codasip is reallocating resources to address the rising demand for cyber-resilient SoCs. The company said digital infrastructure faces more threats, so security should be built into system architecture—not just added by software patches.

Consequently, Codasip is expanding its secure processor portfolio utilizing Capability Hardware Enhanced RISC Instructions (CHERI) technology. The objective is to deliver platforms built for cyber-resilience from the architecture outward, including field-programmable gate arrays and custom silicon. 

“Cyber-resilience has become a strategic requirement for governments, infrastructure operators, and technology providers worldwide,” said Ron Black, CEO of Codasip. “Traditional approaches inefficiently bolt security onto systems after the fact. Our focus is on enabling partners to build security into the fundamental architecture of compute systems from the beginning.”

In conjunction with this move, Codasip announced it is divesting its low-end RISC-V processor design business to an undisclosed U.S. public semiconductor company. As part of this transaction, the acquiring entity will also receive a broad license to Codasip Studio, an EDA tool used for the rapid customization of processor cores.

The transfer of the Codasip Studio EDA license to a U.S. company sparks discussions about how European-funded innovation and RISC-V intellectual property remain commercially available. Ecosystem leaders actively discuss how to secure long-term independence for the high-performance computing sector and ensure foundational design tools stay accessible to regional developers.

Architectural and roadmap considerations for DARE

“It is very concerning that a company such as Codasip has severe difficulties,” Mateo Valero Cortés, director of the Barcelona Supercomputing Center (BSC) and project coordinator, told EE Times. “Europe needs strong companies with leading technology to achieve true sovereignty.”  

Regarding the DARE project’s path forward, Valero indicated that stakeholders are proactively addressing the matter. “The EU and EuroHPC are aware of the situation and are looking for solutions and alternatives,” he said. “In my opinion, EuroHPC should find another European partner to take Codasip’s place.”

The DARE consortium relies on collaborative engineering to produce computing hardware, including a general-purpose processor (GPP) chiplet designed to interface with the OpenChip vector accelerator and the Axelera AI processing unit. Codasip’s shift away from high-performance, general-purpose design raises questions about how the consortium will mitigate architectural friction. The consortium must ensure that the GPP meets requirements for data throughput and low-latency memory access to prevent bottlenecks for the associated accelerators.

Furthermore, introducing a new GPP architecture or modifying the design could lead to roadmap adjustments, including changes to the software co-design efforts led by the BSC and the Jülich Supercomputing Centre. The team must also consider financial and restructuring elements within the €240 million DARE SGA1 project budget.

The consortium must address costs for restructuring the grant agreement, potentially onboarding a replacement GPP partner, and ensuring overall financial stability.

The DARE consortium maintains a measured outlook. In a recent partner update, the organization confirmed ongoing dialogue with Codasip and EuroHPC JU to manage the transition. DARE reiterated its commitment to project goals and noted that all activities continue as planned while it evaluates next steps and manages the transition within the project.

Investment realities and the ecosystem response

The challenges faced by semiconductor companies often stem from the funding environment. During a press briefing on April 15 in Barcelona, Cesc Guim, CEO of Barcelona-based OpenChip, a spinoff of the BSC and a partner in the DARE project, shared insights on the structural differences between European and American investment cultures. He observed that European investors often scrutinize immediate cash flow, which is difficult for semiconductor startups to demonstrate given the multi-year development cycles of custom chips.

In contrast, capital in the U.S. is often deployed more rapidly, driven by foundational ideas and technological potential. Guim noted that if Europe intends to cultivate semiconductor companies, investment processes must become faster, more agile, and less risk-averse.

Guim expressed empathy for Codasip’s situation. “It’s sad for Europe that that’s happening to Codasip, to be honest,” Guim stated. “We try to help them as much as we can in that… try to see how we can work together.”

OpenChip, which began with gap funding from the European Commission and has grown to employ over 300 people, focuses on building a credible business plan backed by early customer engagement. Guim detailed that technology companies must focus heavily on business acumen to ensure financial viability, rather than relying solely on technical merit.

To foster a more robust environment for chip designers, Guim advocates mechanisms from European governing bodies that quickly match private investments with public funds to mitigate the risk-averse nature of regional investors.

Furthermore, Roger Espasa, CEO of Semidynamics, highlighted the fundamental need for unified action among European companies during another press briefing. Asked about Codasip, he argued that many companies fail because they lack fundamental business skills in both the private and public sectors. He also mentioned that Codasip is fundamentally an EDA company, and he feels that Europe needs more players in that field.

Codasip’s strategic divestment and operational pivot introduce variables for the DARE consortium, but its partners said they are seeking alternatives to demonstrate the ecosystem’s resilience.

The shared commitment to cultivating RISC-V intellectual property and secure processing capabilities forms a central pillar of Europe’s technological framework. Ongoing discussions among consortium members and regional alliances show their collective focus on long-term stability.

See also:

Codasip Faces Sale: Pivotal Moment for EU RISC-V Sovereignty

European Project Gets $260 Million for HPC Chip Sovereignty

Is Europe Securing the Wrong Chips?

Codasip: Toward Custom, Safe, Secure RISC-V Compute Cores

data.title: TSMC Chases Soaring AI Demand
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

TSMC is boosting spending plans as it chases soaring AI demand and outpaces its chipmaking rivals.

The world’s largest chip foundry this week said it expects to spend nearly $56 billion this year as it “invests heavily” in production capacity to meet demand from AI customers such as Nvidia, AMD, and Apple. Even at that increased level of spending, TSMC didn’t deny that it will still probably fall short of demand in 2027.

“It takes two to three years to build a new fab, no shortcuts,” CEO C.C. Wei said on a conference call this week to announce first-quarter 2026 results. “It takes another one to two years to ramp it up.”

The company said it will rush construction of three new fabs in Japan, Taiwan, and the U.S. to make 3-nm chips. The Taiwan facility will start production in the first half of 2027, followed by the U.S. site in late 2027 and Japan in 2028.

The company said that, for the first time, it will expand 3-nm production, a node that’s been established for several years. It’s a process technology where the company has no rivals.

“Historically, we do not add additional capacity to a node once it has reached its target capacity,” Wei said. “We are now executing a global capacity plan to support the robust multi-year pipeline of demand for 3-nm technologies, which are used by smartphones, HPC AI—including HBM-based dies—automotive, and IoT customers.”

TSMC started production of the world’s most advanced 2-nm chips late last year, leading competitors such as Samsung and Intel. Still, TSMC’s rivals are sharpening their edge, according to Handel Jones, CEO at International Business Strategies.

“TSMC will continue to be the leader in technology and market share, but the performance of Samsung has improved significantly,” Jones told EE Times.

The large financial resources of Samsung’s memory and logic businesses, in addition to its branded electronics operations, provide an advantage of scale. Profits in the memory business this year are soaring.

“The memory revenues of Samsung will be over $200 billion in 2026, and operating income will be $120 billion or more,” Jones predicted.

Samsung is also catching up in process technology.

“Samsung’s SF2P [second-generation 2 nm] is becoming competitive with N2 [TSMC’s 2-nm tech] in power, performance, and area,” Jones said. “Samsung has obtained a number of high-volume customers and will be capacity-limited.”

Samsung, the world’s second-ranked foundry, will probably more than double its production capacity in Austin, Texas, to 50,000 wafers per month, Jones predicted. The company is adding another 20,000 wafers per month in South Korea and increasing its internal consumption of 2-nm wafers, he said.

Still, TSMC’s rivals won’t take back market share in any meaningful way until 2028, according to Sravan Kundojjala, an analyst at SemiAnalysis.

“TSMC has a more-than five-year head start on N3/N2 yield that competitors cannot compress,” Kundojjala told EE Times. “TSMC’s foundry revenue share went from 54% in 2020 to 72% in 2026, and the gap is still widening.”

Samsung stumbled at the 3-nm node more than two years ago. Intel, revamping a year after new CEO Lip-Bu Tan took the helm, could take some business off TSMC’s hands in advanced packaging. TSMC doesn’t rule out the possibility of opening its compute die to competitors such as Intel. There’s plenty of business to share, Wei said.

“We understand that our competitors also offer very attractive technology, but we welcome that so our customers can have more choices,” Wei said on the conference call. “Then, we can do more business with our customers.”

Intel has very good opportunities in packaging, Jones said.

Intel’s EMIB for advanced packaging is a real technology, but the question is ecosystem and volume, Kundojjala said.

“TSMC holds over 50% of CoWoS capacity through Nvidia alone,” he said. “The challenge for Intel is that customers want front-end wafers and back-end packaging from the same vendor for yield co-optimization. Splitting the two creates integration risk.”

TSMC is adopting new tech for panel-level packaging, which replaces round silicon wafers with square panels. The company is building a pilot line for its CoPoS [Chip-on-Panel-on-Substrate] tech that will probably start production in a few years, Wei said. CoPoS helps increase the density of large AI chiplets and HBM stacks that exceed reticle limits.

ASE, TSMC’s packaging partner, said earlier this year it’s ramping up panel-level packaging for the first time.

Shortages

Shortages of advanced technology wafers and packaging will persist through 2030, according to Jones.

“In 2030, demand for 2 nm and below will be 400K to 450K wafers per month,” he predicted. “Capacity will be 300K to 350K wafers per month.”

TSMC said it will start production of its newest node, A14, in 2028. Compared with TSMC’s 2 nm, A14 will provide up to 15% speed improvement at the same power or up to 30% power improvement at the same speed and close to 20% chip density gain, the company claimed.

“Our A14 technology and its derivatives will further extend our technology leadership position and enable TSMC to capture the growth opportunities well into the future,” Wei said.

In addition to cleanroom construction, several constraints limit TSMC’s ability to increase production, according to Kundojjala.

“ASML’s scarce EUV capacity now has multiple customers,” he said. “Non-EUV tools from Applied Materials, Lam, KLA, and TEL are also capacity-constrained, though lead times are shorter compared to EUV. The Middle East situation is creating price pressure on helium, neon, and specialty chemicals, and Taiwan’s LNG supply chain is being stress-tested. Of these three, equipment supply is the binding constraint through 2027.”

Musk’s Terafab

Wei brushed off the plan of Elon Musk to build TeraFab, a semiconductor complex in Texas, backed by Musk’s Tesla, SpaceX, and xAI, targeting new capacity for 2-nm logic, memory, advanced packaging, and a mask shop in record time on a tight budget.

“Intel and Tesla, they are TSMC’s customers, but again, they are our competitors, and we view Intel as our formidable competitor,” Wei said. “The fundamental rules of the foundry game never change. They need the technology leadership, manufacturing excellence, and customer trust, and most of all, the service.”

See also:

Three Misconceptions About the $402B Semiconductor Foundry Industry

The Silicon Hegemon
